Revelation closes not with distant speculation but with a vision so concrete, so real, that John falls to his knees—and the angel must stop him from worshiping the wrong being. What John has seen is the end of all things: a city where God's presence fills everything, where the curse that has poisoned creation since Genesis is finally lifted, where His servants see His face directly. The river of life flows from the throne of God and the Lamb together, a detail that refuses to let us separate what Christ has accomplished from the Father's purpose. There is no temple in this city because worship is no longer mediated or distant—God Himself dwells openly with His people. The leaves of the tree are for the healing of the nations, suggesting not an end to diversity but an end to division, pain, and the fracture sin created. This is not escapism from the world but the world as God always intended it to be.

Yet between this vision and its fulfillment stands the present moment, and Christ's repeated declaration: "I am coming soon." The angel refuses John's worship and redirects him—worship God, not the messenger—then commands him to leave the prophecy unsealed, open to the churches right now. Time is near. This is not permission to passivity but a call to live differently in light of what's coming. Verses 11 and 14 paint the stark division: let the unrighteous remain unrighteous, and let the righteous practice righteousness. The choice hardens. There is no neutral ground. Those who have washed their robes in the blood of the Lamb will enter the city; those outside—the sexually immoral, the idolaters, those who love falsehood—will remain outside. The invitation is open: "Let the one who is thirsty come." But not everyone will.

Jesus Himself now speaks, claiming titles that belong only to God: Alpha and Omega, First and Last. He is the Root and Offspring of David—both source and descendant, both the foundation of David's line and its fulfillment. He is the bright Morning Star, the herald of a new day. The Spirit and the bride cry "Come," and the book ends not with certainty achieved but with longing expressed, with John's answering cry: "Come, Lord Jesus!" Between the vision and its arrival, grace sustains the saints. The final warning is severe—adding to or subtracting from these words brings judgment—because what is at stake is not a book but reality itself, the redemption of all things. Christ is coming. The question is not whether, but whether you will be among those who say "Come" or among those left outside, unwilling to drink freely from the water of life offered to all.
